coredata.dc:title: Excellent corrosion inhibition and lubrication properties of carbon dot-based ionic liquids as water-based additives
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haocheng
Li, Yi
Zhang, Songwei
Che, Qinglun
Hu, Litian
Zhang, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: Ionic liquids (ILs) and carbon dots (CDs) have gained more attention as versatile materials. Noticeably, the carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs), a novel ionic combination of the two materials, will be endowed with superior performance and promote the application of CDs in fields of corrosion inhibition and lubrication. In this work, three CDILs with different alkyl chain numbers were synthesized as water-based multifunctional additives. CDILs show excellent corrosion inhibition effect in corrosive aqueous solution and superior lubricating behavior in water. Interestingly, CDILs with medium numbers of alkyl chains in cationic moiety exhibits optimal corrosion inhibition and lubrication performance. Both the physical adsorption of CDILs and the structures of cations influence the quality of adsorption and lubricating film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot-based ionic liquids
Corrosion inhibition
Tribological behavior
Water-based additives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nyquist plots of the mild steel in 1 M HCl solution: (a) [N44HH][CDs], (b) [N444H][CDs], (c) [N4444][CDs], (d) 0.5 wt% CDILs.
Equivalent electric circuit for fitting the impedance data.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of the mild steel in 1 M HCl solution: (a) [N44HH][CDs], (b) [N444H][CDs], (c) [N4444][CDs], (d) 0.5 wt% CDILs.
(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) Wear volumes of different concentrations of [N444H][CDs] as additives in water. (Test condition: load of 100 N, temperature of 30 °C, duration of 30 min).
(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) Wear volumes of 1% CDILs as additives in water. (Test condition: load of 100 N, temperature of 30 °C, duration of 30 min).

SEM images of wear scars of disk lubricated by water and water with 1 wt% CDILs: (a-b) Water, (c-d) [N44HH][CDs], (e-f) [N444H][CDs], (g-h) [N4444][CDs] (Test condition: load of 100 N, temperature of 30 °C, duration of 30 min).
SEM images of the corrosion surface on the mild steel: (a) Polished, (b) Blank (1 M HCl), (c) CDs, (d) [N44HH][CDs], (e) [N444H][CDs], (f) [N4444][CDs].
XPS spectra of steel corrosion surface after immersing.

The photographs of solution: (a) the solution before and after immersing under the sunlight, (b) the solution before and after immersing under the UV light, (c) the solution before and after adding the corrosion products under the UV light (Fe3+ or Fe2+). (Left: before, right: after).
Langmuir adsorption isotherm plot of the CDILs on the mild steel interface: (a) [N44HH][CDs], (b) [N444H][CDs], (c) [N4444][CDs].
Schematic illustration of corrosion inhibition mechanism of CDILs.
The Raman points spectra of worn surface.
The XPS spectra of worn surface lubricated by water with 1% [N444H][CDs].
Schematic illustration of lubrication mechanism of CDILs.
The element content (wt%) on polished surface and corrosion surface.

head.ce:title: Excellent corrosion inhibition and lubrication properties of carbon dot-based ionic liquids as water-based additives

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ionic liquids (ILs) and carbon dots (CDs) have gained more attention as versatile materials. Noticeably, the carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs), a novel ionic combination of the two materials, will be endowed with superior performance and promote the application of CDs in fields of corrosion inhibition and lubrication. In this work, three CDILs with different alkyl chain numbers were synthesized as water-based multifunctional additives. CDILs show excellent corrosion inhibition effect in corrosive aqueous solution and superior lubricating behavior in water. Interestingly, CDILs with medium numbers of alkyl chains in cationic moiety exhibits optimal corrosion inhibition and lubrication performance. Both the physical adsorption of CDILs and the structures of cations influence the quality of adsorption and lubricating film.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The equipment failure and energy consumption are inevitable in industrial process. Corrosion and wear are the primary sources to cause above problems [1]. Thus, effective corrosion inhibition and lubrication measures could improve efficiency and save energy [2]. In the lubrication field, oil-based lubricating materials added corrosion inhibitors are often used to control friction and corrosion [3,4]. These oil-based lubricants have good lubricating performance, however, there are some limits including biodegradability, biotoxicity and poor cooling performance [5]. For this, water-based lubricants, as a kind of promising and green lubricants, possess a series of outstanding properties, such as fine colling performance, low cost, high safety and great environmental friendliness [6]. However, it also has disadvantages containing lower viscosity, relatively high surface tension and bad corrosion resistance, which limit its lubricating applications [7]. Therefore, effective additives need to be introduced to enhance the corrosion inhibition property and tribological property in water-based lubricants.

Ionic liquids (ILs) have attracted more and more attention in lubrication since the first research as lubricants in 2001 [8]. Amounts of studies prove that ILs exhibit excellent lubricating properties [9–15]. With the development of ionic liquids research, the halogen element could cause the corrosion wear on the steel surface under the influence of water [16]. Thus, the ILs contained halogen element are not suitable for lubricating on steel surface. Meanwhile, some of these ILs could suppress corrosion in corrosive system, owing to the strong adsorption of cationic or anionic moiety. For instance, the 6-dimethyl-2-mercaptopyrimidine (DMMP) based and benzotriazole (BTA) based ionic liquids adsorb on the steel surface to exploit great anti-corrosion and lubricating abilities [17]. Carbon dots (CDs) have also received a lot of attention since the first discovery in 2004 [18], which could apply in bioimaging [19], chemical additive [20,21], lubrication [22] and so on. Due to the small size and good adsorption, CDs show good friction reducing and anti-wear properties as lubricant additives [23,24]. Besides, some researchers found that CDs are a kind of inhibitor with high inhibition efficient [25–31]. For example, CDs synthesized by ammonium citrate as additives in water resist corrosion and wear remarkably [25]. A new type of N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by using folic acid and o-phenylenediamine could reach 95.4% inhibition efficiency in 1 M HCl solution [29]. Hence, ILs and CDs are hopeful multifunctional additives for water-based lubricants to enhance the performance of corrosion resistance and lubrication.

Based on the above discussion, the carbon dot-based ionic liquids (CDILs) are supposed to exhibit great lubricating and corrosion inhibiting properties, on account of the carbon dot moiety and ionic structure. Based on previous work [32], CDILs could exhibit great lubricating performance as lubricants. It could combine the features of CDs and ILs to improve the performance of CD-based materials. Hence, the water solubility CDILs are ideal for using as multifunctional water-based additives. In this work, three CDILs with different alkyl chain numbers were obtained and used as water-based additives. The electrochemical and tribological tests were carried out to investigate corresponding properties in 1 M HCl solution and water solution, respectively. Besides, studies found that the alkyl chain numbers of cations have a significant impact on corrosion inhibition and lubrication properties. The relationships between cation structures and mechanism of corrosion inhibition and lubrication were also discussed, which might facilitate the design of high-performance water-based additives.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA) was purchased from Tianjin Aaopusheng Chemical Ltd. Dibutylamine (DBA, 99%) and hydrochloric acid (AR, 36 ∼ 38%) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The deionized water was prepared by an ultrapure water purifier in our lab. All the chemicals were afforded as received without further purification. CDs and CDILs were obtained by our previous work [32] (see Supplementary Material for characterization (Fig. S1 to S4: molecular structure, UV–vis, FTIR, TG and DSC)).

The lubricating properties of CDILs as additives of water lubricant were evaluated with an Optimol SRV-V oscillating reciprocating friction and wear tester. The contact model is ball-on-disk friction pairs. The upper running ball (Φ10 mm) and the lower stationary disk (Φ24 mm×8.0 mm) were made by the GCr15 bearing steel. The roughness of the ball and the discs are 25 nm. The tribological tests were conducted at 30 °C under 100 N (frequency: 25 Hz, amplitude: 1.0 mm). The MicroXAM-800 3D surface mapping microscope profilometer was used to measure the wear volume of lower steel disk. Before the tribological tests, the ball and disc were cleaned for 2 min by ultrasonic cleaner and the test surface was wiped with ethanol. The tribological tests were repeated three times for each sample.

Electrochemical experiments (Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Potentiodynamic polarization) were conducted on Gamry Reference 3000 advanced electrochemical workstation. It is a conventional three electrode system which contains reference electrode (mercurous sulfate), counter electrode (platinum) and working electrode (mild steel). The corrosion test system is the 1 M HCl solution with the different concentrations of CDILs and the exposed surface area of mild steel is 1.0 cm2. Before testing, to reach a steady state, the mild steel needs to immerse in the system for one hour at open circuit potential (OCP). The EIS was recorded at the OCP (frequency range: 105 Hz ∼ 10−1 Hz, perturbation amplitude: 5 mV). Then, Potentiodynamic polarization test was also performed at the OCP (potential range: −350 mV ∼ 350 mV, scan rate: 0.5 mV/s). The test data were analyzed and fitted by Gamry Echem. Analyst software.

The morphologies and chemical compositions of the corrosion and worn surface were detected by the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, MERLIN Compact) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI 5000 Versaprobe III) was used to analyze the worn surface with monochromatized Al Ka as the X-ray source. Raman spectra of the worn surface was obtained by the laser Raman spectrometer (Raman, Thermo DXR2, America). The Raman tests were carried out using the Ar laser source (532 nm). Before the characterization test, the samples were cleaned for 10 s by ultrasonic cleaner and the test surface was wiped with ethanol.

The corrosion inhibition behaviors of CDILs were detected by the EIS and Potentiodynamic polarization. Fig. 1 presents the Nyquist plots with diverse concentrations and kinds CDILs. The shape of depressed capacitive arcs in Nyquist diagrams has no significant change as CDILs added into the 1 M HCl solution, indicating that the interface reaction mechanism dominated by charge transfer does not change. Meanwhile, the diameter of capacitive arcs becomes larger after adding the corrosion inhibitor CDILs. With the increase of the additive concentration, the size of capacitive arcs also enlarges. At the concentration of 0.5 wt%, the impedance modulus of [N444H][CDs] is largest among the CDILs. The acquired results reveal that CDILs could retard the corrosion of steel.

The impedance parameters were obtained by using the equivalent electric circuit ( Fig. 2) to fit EIS results, as shown in Table 1. About the model of circuits, the symbols of R s, R p represent solution resistance and polarization resistance, respectively [26,29]. Using the constant phase element (CPE) could get more accurate fitting results compared with double layer capacitance. The impedance of CPE is given through the equation [17,31]:
Z CPE =
1
Y 0
( j ω )
n

In this equation, the Y 0, j and ω are corresponding to the CPE constant, imaginary unit and angular frequency, respectively. And the values of n mean the phase shift. The polarization resistance (R p) can be gained by analyzing the fitting results. Then the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η EIS) after adding CDILs in 1 M HCl solution is calculated by the following formula [17,31]:
η EIS =
R p −
R
p
0
R p
× 100 %
Where R
0
p is the polarization resistance without corrosion inhibitors.

In Table 1, the values of R p and η EIS increase with the increasing addition concentration of CDILs, indicating that the adsorption film generated by inhibitors will become completer and more stable. The inhibition efficiency is over 99% when the concentration is 0.5 wt% for [N444H][CDs] and [N4444][CDs] as additives. Also, decreased Y 0 can be related to the increase of double layers thickness and/or decrease of local dielectric constant, being explained that adsorbed inhibitor molecules on the steel surface gradually replace the water and hydrochloric acid molecules. It could conclude that the inhibition effect of [N4444][CDs] and [N444H][CDs] as inhibitors is better than that of [N44HH][CDs].

Fig. 3 displays the potentiodynamic polarization curves with different corrosion inhibitors in 1 M HCl solution on mild steel. The electrochemical parameters (corrosion potential (E corr), corrosion current density (I corr), corrosion inhibition efficiency (η), anodic and cathodic Tafel slope(θ)) related to these curves are shown in Table 2. The inhibition efficiency (η Tafel) and surface coverage (θ Tafel) of inhibitors are carried out by the equation [17,31] (I corr (with inhibitors) and I 0 corr (without inhibitors)):
η Tafel =
(
1 −
I corr
I
corr
0
)
× 100 %
θ Tafel =
η Tafel / 100 %

The parallel cathodic polarization curves with adding inhibitors in Fig. 3 imply that the inhibition mechanism of inhibitors to the mild steel is not changed. Besides, in Table 2, the E corr of CDILs inhibitors in 1 M HCl solution shifts to negative, and displacements are all less than 85 mV. This reveals that the type of CDILs inhibitors in solution is mixed. Further, as the inhibitors concentration builds up in solution, the values of I corr tend to decrease and η Tafel enhance. This phenomenon may due to the blocking effect of the reaction sites with cathodic and anodic at higher concentration, which hinder the electrochemical reactions. These results suggest the suppression of corrosion on the mild steel. Therefore, at a range of concentrations, the more inhibitors add, the completer and more stable adsorption layer is.

By comparison, at lower concentration (0.2 wt%), the η EIS and η Tafel of [N4444][CDs] could reach a higher level over 90%, while that of [N444H][CDs] and [N44HH][CDs] are lower. This illustrates that [N4444][CDs] with most alkyl chains tends to protect the mild steel at lower concentration. When the concentration increases, [N444H][CDs] achieves higher corrosion inhibition efficiency, while the impedance and anodic curves slope of [N44HH][CDs] change slightly. Overall, CDILs with more alkyl chains ([N4444][CDs] and [N444H][CDs]) display more effective corrosion inhibition in acid solution.

First, the friction tests were experienced with various concentrations of [N444H][CDs] as additives in water, evaluating the effects of additive concentration. As shown in Fig. 4, the neat water shows a higher friction coefficient about 0.45 and wear volume. By contrast, the effect of friction reducing lubricated by [N444H][CDs] additives gradually improves with the increasing concentration of additives, until to the 1%. When the dosage of [N444H][CDs] surpasses 1%, the curves of friction coefficient are almost similar, however, the wear volume will increases. Thus, under a load of 100 N at 30 °C, the optimal concentration of [N444H][CDs] additives is 1% with the relative lower friction coefficient and wear volume.

The lubricating properties of three CDILs at the optimal concentration 1% in water were evaluated. The friction coefficient curves and wear volume are depicted in Fig. 5. All the CDILs could reduce the friction coefficient of pure water. [N444H][CDs] with three alkyl chains exhibits the best friction reducing performance among the CDILs. For other two CDILs, [N44HH][CDs] performs relatively low values in the first 400 s, and then increases to the same level with [N4444][CDs]. As for the wear volume of three CDILs in Fig. 5 b, the anti-wear ability of [N44HH][CDs] is relative poorer than other two. Additionally, [N444H][CDs] produces the lowest wear volume and average friction coefficient among three CDILs. These results may be due to the difference of cationic structure, affecting the strength of water-based lubrication film.

In Fig. 6 , the improvement of CDILs on the surface topography cannot be obviously detected by SEM surface analysis. The worn surface lubricated by neat water shows mass furrows and corrosion pits, implying that severe abrasive wear and corrosion wear occur in the friction process. With the addition of CDILs, furrows decrease and corrosion pits have disappeared, yet cracks and peeling pits appear on the worn surface. These cracks and peeling pits could attribute to the double effect of adsorption film. On the one hand, the adsorption film avoids direct contact with friction pairs to reduce the friction and wear. On the other hand, the CD anions in the adsorption film as polar molecule promote surface cracks extending. Under the fatigue failure of abrasive wear, the cracks gradually exfoliate from the surface to form peeling pits. The similar worn surface is also occurred in other water-based lubrication system [14,33].

According to the electrochemical measurements data, the corrosion inhibition effect of CDILs with same CD anions is discrepant, which could be attributed to the cations. Consequently, the EIS and potentiodynamic polarization curves of CDs as additives are tested. It can be seen from the Fig. S5 that the CDs without ionic modification have little corrosion inhibition effect as additives. This indicates that not only the ionic modification could enhance the corrosion inhibition ability of CDs, but also the molecule structures of cations have influence on the effect.

To see the macro corrosion inhibition effect, the immersion test was carried out. The SEM images of mild steel surface (magnification: 200 times) immersed in 1 M HC1 solution with different inhibitors for 24 h are displayed in Fig. 7. The surface of polished steel only exists some scratches without obvious coverings and corrosion pits (Fig. 7 a). After immersing in the acid solution, the image of blank solution (1 M HC1 solution, Fig. 7 b) shows plenty of corrosion products with many cracks and pits, implying that severe corrosion has occurred on the steel surface. In contrast, when adding 0.5 wt% [N444H][CDs] or [N4444][CDs] (Figs. 7 e and 7 f), it could still find the polishing grinding traces and there is slight corrosion on the surface. However, [N44HH][CDs] and CDs (Figs. 7 c and 7 d) as inhibitors do not have good corrosion inhibition effect, which are little better than blank solution. The EDS results of corrosion surface are displayed in Table 3. In the blank solution, owing to the dissolution of Fe and oxidation of steel surface, the weight fraction of Fe decreases and the O increases by comparison with polished surface. Nevertheless, the extent of decrease and increase significantly reduces after adding inhibitors. To further analyze the composition of corrosion surface, the XPS spectra of the mild steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution with and without 0.5 wt% [N444H][CDs] were obtained ( Fig. 8). By observation, the corrosion surface mainly consist of the element C, O and Fe except a small amount of N in the spectra of [N444H][CDs]. Compared with blank sample, the increased element C, decreased element O and detected element N confirm that CDILs adsorb on the surface to form protective film.

Comparing these photographs of solution before and after immersing tests ( Fig. 9 a), the color of solutions after immersing turns yellow, manifesting the formation of corrosion products (Fe2+ oxidated to Fe3+ in air). The color of blank solution without inhibitors is more yellow than CDILs, which reveals the intense corrosion. Meanwhile, under the light with UV lamp (Fig. 9 b), the fluorescence of CDILs disappears after immersing while there is no fluorescence in the blank solution. This might illustrate that (1) a part of CDILs is consumed to adsorb on the steel surface, (2) the interactions happened between corrosion products (Fe3+) with CDILs inhibitors lead to quenching reaction, such as electron transfer and coordination [34]. The hypothesis (1) has been testified by XPS analyses. For the hypothesis (2), previous researches have implied that Fe3+ have a stronger affinity with the group contained oxygen [35]. CD anions have the oxygen-containing groups on the surface, which might cause fluorescence quenching by the interaction between them. However, in the HCl solution, the corrosion products are mainly Fe2+ rather than Fe3+. To evaluate the effect of Fe2+, the corrosion products solution (Fig. S6) after immersing for placing shortly (corrosion products Fe2+) and a long time (Fe3+) was dripped into the CDILs solution to observe the change of fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 9 c, the fluorescence quenching occurs in both samples with the addition of corrosion products. The effect of fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ is stronger than that of Fe2+, implying the higher interaction between CDILs and Fe3+. Results declare that there is partly interaction between CDILs with corrosion products during the corrosion process, enhancing the adsorption on the steel surface. Whereas, this interaction between Fe2+ and CD anions is relatively weaker, so that the inhibition effect is more determined by the physical adsorption of CDILs and the structures of cations.

For purpose of realizing the adsorption mechanism of CDILs on the solution interface, employing the Langmuir adsorption isotherm to fit the electrochemical test results of Tafel curves, as shown in Fig. 10. All the fitted curves are obtained by the following equation [27,28]:
C
θ Tafel
=
1
K ads
+ C

Where θ Tafel and C are the surface coverage and inhibitors concentration; K ads is the adsorptive equilibrium constant. By fitting the results, the values of R2 for [N4444][CDs] (0.99999), [N444H][CDs] (0.99991) and [N44HH][CDs] (0.99993) and the slope of fitted lines (close to 1) demonstrate that the behavior of adsorption is accord with the Langmuir adsorption model. The adsorption free energy (ΔG 0 ads) can be calculated by using the K ads:
Δ
G
ads
0
=
− R T
ln
(
1000 K ads
)

R (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1) is the molar gas constant and T (298 K) is the absolute temperature. The value of 1000 is the concentration of water in solution, which need to be consistent with the unit of K ads (L·g−1).

Usually, the adsorption types are evaluated by the ΔG 0 ads values. While the ΔG 0 ads ≥ −20, it could be considered as physical adsorption. While the ΔG 0 ads ≤ −40, it could be regarded as chemical adsorption. In this work, the values of ΔG 0 ads are − 20.31, − 20.64, − 21.99, respectively. The negative values indicate that the adsorption of CDILs is a spontaneous process. This also deduces that the interaction between CDILs and mild steel is mainly physical adsorption.

As mentioned above, CDILs could provide higher inhibition effect on the mild steel in 1 M HCl aqueous solution, and the inhibition mechanism diagrams are described in Fig. 11. Based on the surface analyses and Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the adsorption type of CDILs on the steel surface is mainly physical adsorption along with less chemical adsorption. Also, according to the previous literature [17,36], inhibitors in the acid corrosion system (HCl or H2SO4) could combine the Fe2+ to form complex compounds, which resist the corrosion attack of H+. In this work, through the fluorescence quenching test, the CDILs molecules have weaker interaction with Fe2+. Thus, it shows partly chemical adsorption on steel surface, which is consistent with Langmuir adsorption. For CDs, the adsorption ability on the steel surface is too weak to form compact protective film, which is probably due to the absence of heteroatoms (N, S) within the CDs. After ionizing the CDs to obtain CDILs, the ionic liquids possess CD anions and ammonium cations, which could easier adsorb on the steel by the electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, the cations are around CDs anions via electrostatic attraction to increase the compactness of adsorption film. Therefore, the protective film formed by CDILs could greatly retards the contact of water and H+ to prevent the corrosion of steel.

At low concentration, the inhibition effect is generally lower for three CDILs, owing to the less adsorbing quantity of CDILs likely. When the addition goes up, there is a significant difference in inhibitory. As a result of the same anions, the cation structures may be supposed to have major influence in this phenomenon. To our knowledge, the alkyl chains are hydrophobic. For [N44HH][CDs], it has the fewest alkyl chain so that the hydrophobic effect is the worst. Thus, the adsorption film obtained by the cations of [N44HH][CDs] is inclined to contact water and H+, for which the steel surface is more susceptible to corrosion. Furthermore, the binding effect between cations and water molecules in aqueous solution also affects the adsorption. [N44HH][CDs] has two alkyl chains and N-H bonds, making it harder to break the binding effect. Under the influence of the two aspects, [N44HH][CDs] exhibits the worst corrosion inhibition effect among the CDILs. In the Tafel curves of [N44HH][CDs] as inhibitors (Fig. 3 d), the corrosion current densities of cathodic branch change slightly compared with the blank curve, however, the other two CDILs change larger at high concentration. Given the previous reaction mechanism [29,37], this change in [N44HH][CDs] curves of also proves the steel surface is easier to contract water and H+.

The worn surface was examined by the Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 12 and Fig. S7. All the spectra have a broadness peak at the range of 650 ∼ 700 cm−1, which is identified as the iron oxides [38,39]. After added CDILs, new carbon Raman signals (1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1) are detected on the worn surfaces, which indicates that CDILs could promote the formation of deposited carbon in the friction process. On further analysis, however, the intensity ratio of iron oxides and deposited carbon on the worn surface lubricated by CDILs as additives in water is different. Obviously, the ratio of iron oxides and deposited carbon for [N44HH][CDs] is the highest among the three spectra (Fig. 12 a), implying their low carbon content in the tribofilm and serious oxidation of steel surface. In other words, the quality of tribofilm is not tough enough, which might be contributed to the poorer adsorption quality. This is also consistent with the corrosion test results. To verify the reliability of this result, another three points were selected for Raman test (Figs. 12 b to 12 d and Table S1). The average intensity ratio of [N44HH][CDs], [N444H][CDs] and [N4444][CDs] is 2.10, 1.48 and 0.96, conforming the tendency of Fig. 12 a. Also, through the EDS element weight content of unworn surface and worn surface by pure water with 1% CDILs (Table S2), the carbon content is increased obviously compared with unworn surface and the oxygen content of [N44HH][CDs] is more higher than other two CDILs.

To further analyze the composition of tribofilm, the XPS spectra of the worn surface lubricated by the water with 1% [N444H][CDs] are displayed in Fig. 13. For the C 1 s peaks located at 284.84 eV, 286.98 eV and 288.27 eV, which are attributed to the C-C/CC, C-O/C-N and CO, respectively. The O 1 s peak at 530.06 eV and Fe 2p peaks are evidence of iron oxides. And N 1 s peaks at 399.70 eV and 406.39 eV are assigned to the C-N of alkyl chain of cations and N-O, implying that slight tribochemical reactions occurred in the friction process between CDILs and substrate [40]. Viewing the survey spectrum, C and O elements are abundant, and N element is not obvious, indicating that the tribofilm mainly consist of deposited carbon and iron oxides obtained by the adsorption and tribochemical reactions.

Together with the friction test results and worn surface analyses, the lubrication mechanism diagrams are represented in Fig. 14. CDILs as additives could improve the lubricity of water-based lubricants by forming the tribofilm contained iron oxide and carbon film. Results suggest that although CDILs have the same CD anions, they show the different lubricating properties as additives in water. It is forcefully indicated that the structures of cations could play an important role in lubricating behavior. For this, based on the corrosion analyses and previous studies [6,14], the role might be affected in two aspects, adsorption layer and hydration layer.

During the friction process, due to electron emissions from the friction surface, similar to other ILs as lubricant additives, CDILs molecules also probably adsorb on the steel surface to form adsorption film initially. This film could gradually transform to tribofilm under friction stress, preventing direct contract between steel ball and disk. The numbers of cationic alkyl chain may have an influence on the adsorption film, such as compactness and stability. According to the corrosion measurements and Raman characterization, as a result of [N44HH][CDs] with two alkyl chains, the compactness and stability of adsorption film are supposed to be the worst among the CDILs. In this case, the adsorption film tends to be destructed so that the tribofilm is difficult to form. While the alkyl chain numbers of [N444H][CDs] and [N4444][CDs] are relatively more, in consequence, their anti-wear property are better than [N44HH][CDs] as additives in water. Especially, although the friction coefficient of [N4444][CDs] is higher, the stronger adsorption film might offset the influence of friction force to express lower wear.

CDILs, as ionic liquids, with many oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CD anions, are able to form hydration layer with water molecules in aqueous solution. The hydration layer could play the role of boundary lubrication film in this lubricating system, reducing the friction in comparison with neat water. For the protic CDILs, the existence of N-H bond on the cations may improve its attraction of water molecules to enhance the lubricity of hydration layer, further decrease the horizontal shear damping. The lubricating behavior of three CDILs aqueous lubricants are mainly depended on the combination of adsorption and hydration layers.

In this work, three carbon dot-based ionic liquids with various alkyl chain numbers were synthesized. The corrosion inhibition and lubrication properties and mechanisms were evaluated in 1 M HCl solution and aqueous solution on the steel, respectively. The conclusions could be obtained as follow:
(1)
CDILs combine the advantages of ILs and CDs, which are the promising additives in water lubricant. They can enhance the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CDs without heteroatoms like N and S.
(2)
CDILs exhibit great corrosion inhibition performance in 1 M HCl solution. [N444H][CDs] and [N4444][CDs] could achieve 99% inhibition efficiency at the concentration of 0.5 wt%. In acid corrosion system, CDILs molecules could form protective layer mainly via physical adsorptions to prevent surface from corroding. In particular, CDILs with more alkyl chain numbers could form more compact protective film, which prevent the dissolution reaction of anodic iron more effectively.
(3)
As water-based lubricant additives, CDILs show excellent friction reducing and anti-wear properties. Their superior lubricating behavior are mainly attributed to the combination of the adsorption film and hydration layer on the frictional pairs.
(4)
The structures of cations play an important role in corrosion inhibition and lubrication properties, for which influence the quality of adsorption film and lubrication film, respectively.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51975559 and U21A20280), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2020ME135 and ZR2022ME200).

ce:list-item.ce:para: The work described has not been submitted elsewhere for publication, and all the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.
I have read and have abided by the statement of ethical standards for manuscripts submitted to Tribology International.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.triboint.2023.108990.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A

.
